docs.title: PBR Quick Reference
docs.text:
A version-controlled system for personal conduct, interpersonal expectations, and self-preservation through a significant life transition. See the Rationale for full context on why this exists and how it works.

  • Basic Dignity Standard: Yelling and coarse dismissal are not acceptable from anyone, in any direction; a shared verbal interrupt is in place to pause and reset when the line is crossed.
  • Request Management & Response / SLA: I utilize a mandatory 24-hour buffer for all non-emergency decisions to ensure my responses align with my actual capacity and priorities.
  • Acts of Service: Service is a chosen act of love, not a social reflex; I will distinguish between what I freely offer and what is asked simply because I am expected to say yes.
  • Role Definition & Domestic Integration: My partner is an allied, independent adult in our home — not a disciplinary figure; parenting responsibilities remain entirely mine, and her right to disengage from household chaos is non-negotiable.
  • Physical Integrity: Physical contact is reserved for fun and play, and I will not be a target for physical touch motivated by frustration or anger.
  • Internal Resource Audit: I maintain a 20% energy buffer for my well-being and do not accept new commitments when my internal "tank" is low.
  • Conflict Isolation: I will not participate in elevated tones or verbal conflict within earshot of the children, moving high-friction topics to a later, private time or digital channel.
  • Co-Parenting & Autonomy: What exists now is a co-parenting partnership defined strictly by the children's welfare; proactive personal or emotional support is no longer part of that role.
  • Disclosure & Heartbeat: During major life events, updates are capped to one concise daily report so the situation doesn't consume the relationship; emotional assurance remains unlimited and always the priority.
  • Information Disclosure: I provide factual, logistical updates on the divorce process, but discussions regarding moral, theological, or personal causes are off-limits.

docs.title: Boundary: Acts of Service — Offered vs. Extracted
1. The Line
docs.text: Service given freely from a place of love is a gift. Service given because someone asked and I know I will say yes to avoid friction is a transaction that depletes me. I will not conflate the two.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: Acts of service are one of the ways I show love and care — that is not the problem. The problem is when the pattern becomes predictable enough that people begin extracting service rather than receiving it. They ask not because I am better positioned to help, but because they know I will not say no. Over time this conditions a dynamic where my helpfulness is treated as a given, declining feels abnormal, and the meaning of the service is quietly eroded. Resentment follows. The act looked the same from the outside, but the internal experience was entirely different.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• The "Either of Us Could" Test: Before acting on a request, I ask: is there a genuine reason this person cannot do this themselves, or are we equally positioned and they are simply asking because they know I will? If the answer is "equally positioned," that is a flag to pause.
  • The Pause: I do not respond immediately to requests for small acts of service. A brief internal check is not rudeness — it is honesty. The result may still be yes, but it will be a chosen yes.
  • The Authentic Response: A calm, unapologetic no is as valid as a yes. Neither requires explanation or justification for routine requests.
  • Recalibrating the Dynamic: Consistently saying yes trains people to always ask. Saying no occasionally — without drama — recalibrates the expectation without confrontation. This is not withholding; it is conditioning a healthier pattern.

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Redirect]: "You're closer than I am — can you grab it?"
  • [SIGNAL - The Honest No]: "I'm comfortable right now. I'd rather not."
  • [SIGNAL - The Chosen Yes]: (only when genuinely freely given) "Sure, I've got it."

Internal Check

If I notice resentment after completing a small act of service, the yes was not freely given. That resentment is data — not a reason to comply more quietly, but a signal to recalibrate. The fix is to say no more often until the dynamic resets.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-CORE-008
status: Draft
severity: Medium
review_frequency: Monthly
scope: Internal / All Relationships

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: Yelling, "shut up," and coarse dismissal are not acceptable forms of communication in this household — from anyone, in any direction, regardless of frustration level.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: This boundary was established to model the interpersonal baseline I want my kids to carry into their own lives. Everyone in this space is owed basic dignity. Frustration is valid; contempt expressed through volume or dismissal is not. This is not about perfection — it is about the floor.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• No Directional Exceptions: The standard applies in every direction — kids to adults, adults to kids, adults to each other. Toughness or rank do not grant an exemption.
  • Frustration Has Other Outlets: Feelings are valid. Yelling and dismissal are not a valid channel for them. Walking away, using words, or invoking the Interrupt Signal are the alternatives.
  • Consistency Over Mood: The standard does not flex based on stress level, history, or who started it.

docs.title: 4. The Interrupt Signal
docs.text:
Either of the following words, spoken by anyone in the household, signals that the Basic Dignity Standard has been violated and the current interaction must pause immediately:

  • pumpernickel
  • perpendicular

When the signal is spoken:

  1. The current exchange stops — no "but I was just..." and no counter-argument.
  2. Everyone takes a physical step back and a breath.
  3. The person who crossed the line acknowledges it simply: "You're right. I'm sorry."
  4. The conversation may resume once the tone has reset.

The signal cannot be dismissed or argued. Whoever speaks it is always valid in doing so.

docs.title: 5. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Interrupt]: (spoken by anyone) "Pumpernickel." / "Perpendicular."
  • [SIGNAL - The Acknowledgment]: "You're right. I'm sorry. Let me try that again."
  • [SIGNAL - The Reset]: "Are we good? Can we start over?"

Internal Check

If the Interrupt Signal is directed at me, I do not argue or minimize it. The signal is correct by definition — the only valid response is to stop and reset.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-CORE-007
status: Active
severity: High
review_frequency: Annual
scope: Internal / Household-wide
audience: All household members

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: What exists now is a co-parenting partnership, defined strictly by the children's welfare. I am no longer a provider of proactive personal, logistical, or emotional support.

docs.title: The Model
docs.text:
There are two ways to frame a co-parenting relationship. The first is adversarial:

Me  ——→  [issues]  ←——  You

Each party faces the issues from opposite sides. Every problem becomes a negotiation between opponents.

The second is a team model:

Me —— coParents —— You
  \                /
   \              /
       [issues]

We face the issues together, from the same side. The children's welfare is the shared objective — not a bargaining chip, not a battlefield.

This boundary is built on the team model. The relationship between us changed. The team did not.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: The decision to divorce necessitates a redistribution of internal resources. To ensure the stability of the children and my own mental health, I must prioritize self-care and individual autonomy over traditional marital habits of service.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• Child-First Protocol: All requests or actions related to the children's health, education, or well-being take absolute priority over all other communications.
  • Request-Only Assistance: I will not "mind-read" or preemptively solve problems. Assistance is strictly on-demand and subject to my current availability.
  • Digital Boundaries: Communication should be restricted to child-related logistics. Personal life updates or "venting" will be met with the [SIGNAL] below.
  • Task Separation: Household, automotive, or administrative tasks for your residence are no longer my responsibility.

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - Emotional Support]: "I understand this is difficult, but we are no longer each other's emotional support system. I need you to lean on your friends or family for this."
  • [SIGNAL - Proactive Expectation]: "I wasn't aware you needed help with that. Moving forward, please send a specific request rather than assuming I will step in."
  • [SIGNAL - Priority Check]: "Right now, I am focusing on the kids' needs. If this isn't about them, I'll have to get back to you later (or not at all)."

Enforcement Step

If the boundary between "Co-parent" and "Partner" is blurred, I will pause the conversation and resume it only once the topic returns to the children's welfare.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-REL-001
status: Active
severity: High
review_frequency: Event-Driven (Legal Milestones)
scope: Relational / Post-Divorce
audience: Co-Parent

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: I will not participate in any verbal conflict, elevated tones, or "high-friction" arguments while the children are physically present or within earshot.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: Children are hyper-sensitive to parental "system instability." Exposure to conflict causes elevated cortisol and long-term emotional damage. Isolating conflict to adult-only environments preserves the kids' safety layer and keeps the co-parenting dynamic professional in their eyes.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• The "Immediate Mute" Rule: If any conversation begins to escalate (raised voices, sarcasm, rapid-fire interruptions) while the kids are present, I will immediately stop speaking.
  • The Physical Buffer: If the other party continues to escalate, I will move to a different room or my vehicle.
  • The Asynchronous Hand-off: All "High-Friction" topics must be moved to P3/P4 channels (Email/App) as per BND-OPS-003. High-friction topics do not get resolved in the driveway.
  • The "Wait Out": I will not respond to baiting or provocations intended to spark a reaction in front of the kids.

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Immediate Halt]: "The kids are right here. I am stopping this conversation now. We can discuss this via email tomorrow morning."
  • [SIGNAL - The De-escalation]: "I'm not going to yell. I'm going to take the kids [to the park / inside / to the car] now. Let's touch base when they aren't around."
  • [SIGNAL - The Boundary Reminder]: "The policy is 'Zero-Audience.' I'm hanging up/walking away now because the kids can hear us."

Enforcement Step

If escalation continues after the Immediate Halt signal and the other party follows me to continue the argument in front of the kids, I will leave the premises entirely (if safe for the kids) or call a "Time Out" and refuse to engage until that person has physically left the environment.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-REL-004
status: Active
severity: Critical
review_frequency: Post-Incident
scope: Co-Parenting / Environment Isolation
audience: Co-Parent (primary); any adult in the children's environment

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: When I disclose difficult information regarding my divorce or family of origin and invoke the 24-hour rule, I am asking for a window of active listening only — no advice, critiques, or "fix-it" responses until that window closes.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: As I move away from a history of "peace-keeping," I am learning to sit with my own discomfort. When my partner offers an immediate opinion or emotional reaction, my reflexive "fix-it" bias is triggered. This forces me to stop processing my own feelings to manage hers. I need a "sandbox" where I can speak without immediate external feedback.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• The "Notice of Disclosure": I will preface sensitive shares with: "I need to share something, but I am invoking the 24-hour rule."
  • Active Listening Only: During the 24-hour window, the goal is "Witnessing," not "Solving."
  • The "Holding Tank": If my partner has an opinion or an urge to help, she is encouraged to write it down or process it elsewhere, rather than sharing it with me immediately.
  • Consent to Advise: After 24 hours, the "Write-Lock" is lifted, but advice should still be preceded by: "Are you ready for some feedback on what you shared yesterday?"

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Intake]: "I have some hard news about the divorce. I need to tell you so you're in the loop, but I need us to observe the 24-hour rule so I can just sit with it for a bit."
  • [SIGNAL - The Interruption]: "I know you're trying to help and I love that, but right now my 'fix-it' brain is turning on. Can we put a pin in that advice until tomorrow?"
  • [SIGNAL - The Appreciation]: "Thank you for just listening and not jumping in. It makes me feel safe to keep sharing these updates with you."

Internal Enforcement

If I feel myself trying to "calm her down" regarding a situation that is happening to me, I will pause the conversation. It is not my job to manage her emotional reaction to my life's challenges.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-REL-003
status: Active
severity: High
review_frequency: Monthly (Check-in)
scope: Current Relationship / Intimacy

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: During elevated-stress periods, this protocol can be invoked to prevent any single external situation from consuming the relational space between us — capping updates while keeping assurances high.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: Most of the time, no special structure is needed. When a sustained external situation generates significant noise — a legal filing, a family event, a difficult co-parenting stretch — this protocol protects relational bandwidth without requiring constant renegotiation in the moment. The cap on updates is temporary and situational; the assurances are not.

docs.title: 3.1 The Status Report (When Invoked)
docs.text:
• Trigger: Invoked during a defined high-friction period, not as a standing daily rule.
  • Format: One concise factual update per day — "The What," not "The Feel."
  • Buffer: Any "Hard Disclosures" within the window still follow BND-REL-003 (the 24-hour rule).

docs.title: 3.2 The Heartbeat Protocol (Always On)
docs.text:
• Explicit Assurance: Use high-certainty language to signal relationship security, especially when preoccupied.
  • Active Check-ins: During high-stress events, initiate brief "System Checks" to confirm priority.
  • Keyword Phrases:
    • "You are non-negotiable. Full stop."
    • "I am dealing with [Issue], but my commitment to us is unchanged."

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - Invoking the Cap]: "This [situation] is generating a lot of noise right now. I'd like to invoke the daily-update cap so it doesn't take over our time together — one update a day, then I'm closing that tab."
  • [SIGNAL - The Heartbeat]: "I know I'm a bit distracted by [situation] right now, but I want you to know: You are my person. You are non-negotiable."

Internal Check

If providing assurance starts to feel like a chore, it is a signal that the external situation has taken up too much space — not that the assurance itself is the problem. Step back, not away.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-REL-003.1
status: Active
severity: Medium
review_frequency: Event-Driven
scope: Current Relationship / Maintenance

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: I provide factual, logistical updates regarding the divorce process; I do not engage in discussions regarding the moral, theological, or "inner-working" causes of the separation.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: To preserve the health of my familial relationships while navigating a significant life transition. Discussing the "why" or the alignment of my choices with evangelical values creates conflict and emotional exhaustion rather than helpful support.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• Allow-listed Topics:
    • Housing and living arrangements.
    • Co-parenting schedules and kids' well-being.
    • Legal timelines and filing milestones.
  • Block-listed Topics:
    • Theological "covenant" status or reconciliation potential.
    • The "cause" or "who started it" narratives.
    • Current personal values or religious deconstruction.
    • Accusations or speculation (e.g., affairs, "falling away").
  • Active Filtering: If a conversation shifts from logistics to judgment, I will apply the [SIGNAL] immediately.

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Pivot]: "I appreciate that you want to be a listening ear, but for our relationship to stay healthy, I'm only sharing the 'what' and 'when' of this process, not the 'why'."
  • [SIGNAL - Theological Deflection]: "I know your faith is important to you and you're coming from a place of concern, but I am not open to discussing the religious or moral aspects of this decision."
  • [SIGNAL - The "Help" Clarifier]: "If you want to help, the best thing you can do is [Specific Task, e.g., help with the kids on Tuesday / check in on my move]. I'm not looking for a place to process the details of the marriage itself."

Enforcement Step

If family members continue to press for "inner workings" after being redirected, I will end the call or leave the room. I will not justify or defend my life choices to those who are using a value system I no longer subscribe to.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-REL-002
status: Active
severity: Medium
review_frequency: Monthly
scope: Relational / Extended Family

docs.title: Boundary: Internal Resource Audit (The Capacity Check)
1. The Line
docs.text: I will not commit to any new expenditure of time, money, or emotional labor without first auditing my current "Resource Tank" and confirming a 20% surplus.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: My default setting is to prioritize external needs to keep the peace. This leads to "Silent Burnout," where I am functionally operational but internally depleted. By requiring a 20% "Safety Buffer," I ensure that I always have enough reserves to handle an emergency with my kids without breaking.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• The "24-Hour Review" Default: This activates BND-OPS-003 for every request, even "fun" ones. No "Yes" is delivered in the moment.
  • The Hard-Stop Sunday: Sunday evenings are a "Read-Only" period. No planning, no heavy chores, no stressful texts. This is a manual reboot for the upcoming week.
  • The "Capacity Filter" Questions: Before agreeing to help or show up, I must answer:
    1. Does this take away from my kids or my sleep?
    2. Am I doing this because I want to or because I'm afraid of the friction of saying no?
    3. If I say yes, what am I saying "No" to in my own life?

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - Internal]: "I feel the urge to say yes right now just to make this person happy. That is a System Alert. I am invoking the 24-hour buffer."
  • [SIGNAL - External]: "That sounds like a great cause/event, but I've reached my 'people capacity' for this week. I need to stay home and recharge so I can be 100% for my kids tomorrow."
  • [SIGNAL - The Correction]: "I previously said I could help with that, but I realized I overcommitted myself. I'm going to have to scale back my involvement to [Specific Limit] to keep my own life balanced."

docs.title: 5. Decision Logic: The Resource Tank
docs.text:
Tank Level Action Protocol High (>80%) Proceed with standard "Decision Latency" (BND-OPS-003). Medium (50–80%) One-in, One-out. (To do something new, cancel something else). Low (<50%) Hard Freeze. No new commitments. Cancel non-essential tasks.

Internal Enforcement

If I find myself feeling resentful toward a person I just helped, it is a sign that I bypassed this audit. I must immediately enter "Maintenance Mode" (Solitude/Rest) to prevent a total system failure.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-CORE-006
status: Active
severity: Critical
review_frequency: Monthly
scope: Internal / Self-Management

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: Physical contact is strictly categorized into "Play" (consensual/joyful) or "Reaction" (frustrated/aggressive); I do not accept "Reaction" contact, regardless of my ability to "handle" the pain.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: Allowing kids to hit out of frustration because "I'm tough" teaches them that physical aggression is an acceptable outlet for anger. To protect their future relationships and my own physical autonomy, I must enforce a "Zero-Grievance Hitting" policy while maintaining the play that builds our bond.

docs.title: 3. Implementation
docs.text:
• The "Hands-Off" Transition: If a game of wrestling or rough-housing shifts from laughter to frustration, the game is immediately terminated.
  • The "Toughness" Decoupling: I will stop using my physical strength as a reason to allow hitting. The rule is based on the intent of the touch, not the impact of the blow.
  • The Interception: When a frustrated hit is attempted, I will physically block/intercept the hand (without counter-aggression) and deploy the [SIGNAL].

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Immediate Halt]: "Hands off. I don't play when we're angry. We are done with this game until we are both calm."
  • [SIGNAL - The Logic Check]: "I'm tough enough to handle that hit, but I'm too important to be hit out of anger. If you're frustrated, use your words or walk away."
  • [SIGNAL - The Re-Entry]: "I'm ready to play again when you can keep your hands in 'fun mode' instead of 'mad mode.' Are you ready yet?"

Enforcement Step

If a hit occurs out of frustration, I will immediately create 5–10 feet of physical distance. I will not lecture while in range of another hit; I will wait until the child's nervous system has cooled down before discussing the boundary.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-CORE-005
status: Active
severity: Medium
review_frequency: Annual
scope: Internal / Parenting

docs.title: 1. The Line
docs.text: I do not provide immediate consent or responses to non-emergency requests; all significant decisions and communications follow a mandatory "Triage and Buffer" protocol.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: To eliminate "Conflict Avoidance Bias" (agreeing just to keep the peace) and to prevent "Notification Fatigue." This policy ensures that my responses are based on capacity and facts, not emotional pressure or the desire for a "quick fix" to a conversation.

docs.title: 3. The Triage & Response Table (The SLA)
docs.text: Priority Definition Response Window Decision Buffer P1: Critical Immediate physical danger or acute health crisis. < 15 Minutes None (Act Now) P2: High Schedule changes for the next 24–48 hours. 4–8 Hours 1-hour "Cool-off" P3: Normal General logistics, future planning, or family info. 24–48 Hours Mandatory 24-hour buffer P4: Low Non-essential updates or "FYIs." Next Weekly Sync N/A Blocked Emotional venting, theological debates, or critiques. Null (No Response) N/A

docs.title: 4.1 The "Wait-Out" Rule
docs.text:
• The 24-Hour Buffer: For any P3 request involving my time, money, or emotional labor, I must wait at least 24 hours before confirming.
  • Channel Integrity: If a P3 message is sent via a P1 channel (e.g., a text about a bill next month), I will move the conversation to the appropriate P3 channel (Email/App) and reset the clock.

docs.title: 4.2 The Decision Flowchart
docs.text:
When a request enters the system:

  1. Identify Priority: Is this a P1 (Medical/Safety)?
  2. Apply Buffer: If not P1, do not commit. State the "Intake Signal."
  3. Internal Audit: Write down the "External Argument" vs. my "Internal Capacity."
  4. Final Response: Deliver a "Yes" or "No" only after the buffer has expired.

docs.title: 5. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - The Intake]: "I hear your request. Since this isn't an emergency, I'm going to take 24 hours to check my schedule and priorities before giving you an answer."
  • [SIGNAL - The Pressure Valve]: "I'm not in a position to make a snap decision. If you need an answer immediately, it will have to be a 'No' so I don't overcommit."
  • [SIGNAL - The Channel Reset]: "I see your message about [Topic]. I'm moving this to email so I can give it proper attention during my logistics window tomorrow."

Internal Enforcement

If I feel a physical "urge to appease" (tight chest, desire to stop the other person from being upset), that is a System Alert. I must exit the conversation or hang up until the buffer period is over.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-OPS-003
status: Active
severity: Critical
review_frequency: Monthly (Self-Audit)
scope: Internal & External / All Stakeholders

docs.title: Boundary: Role Definition & Domestic Integration (Partner & Children)
1. The Line
docs.text: My partner functions as an allied, independent adult in our home — a presence of safety and support for the children, not an authority figure who issues discipline or makes parenting decisions.

docs.title: 2. Rationale
docs.text: To ensure a healthy, sustainable integration of our lives while protecting my partner's autonomy, mental health, and individual identity. Children accept discipline best from their biological parents; forcing a partner into an enforcement role breeds resentment. Conversely, my partner has an absolute right to individual respect and a personal sanctuary within our shared space.

docs.title: 3.1 Discipline & Conflict
docs.text:
• The Supportive Presence: My partner's role with the children is warmth and safety, not authority. She is empowered to comfort, reassure, and provide basic care — especially when I am not present — without that crossing into consequences or parenting decisions.
  • The Hands-Off Rule: My partner will not issue punishments, consequences, or directives to my children.
  • Conflict Non-Intervention: My partner will not step in or break up physical or verbal infighting between the kids. That responsibility is 100% mine — unless physical safety is at immediate risk.
  • The Safety Exception: If bodily harm is imminent and I am not present, my partner will intervene to protect the children. This is not discipline — it is basic protection, and it is always the right call.
  • Injury Response: If a child is injured in my absence, my partner will alert me immediately and take whatever steps are needed to handle it. She does not wait for my return to act.
  • The Escalation Path: For non-safety misbehavior when I am not in the room, my partner will note the behavior and hand it off to me to address upon return.

docs.title: 3.2 Personal Respect & Boundaries
docs.text:
• The Human Threshold: My children must treat my partner with the basic respect owed to any human being. Coarse language (e.g., "shut up," yelling, blatant disrespect) is a direct violation of house rules.
  • The Separation Protocol (Sanity Tool): If my partner feels overwhelmed by the noise, chaos, or energy of the household, she has an absolute, non-negotiable right to separate herself entirely (e.g., leaving the room, going for a walk, retreating to the bedroom). This is explicitly classified as a healthy self-regulation tool, never as a failure to "handle it."

docs.title: 3.3 Autonomy & Identity
docs.text: • Preserving the "Self": Joining our lives does not mean absorbing her identity. My partner is encouraged to maintain her independent hobbies, friendships, and solo routines without guilt or pressure to conform to a "traditional stepmom" mold.

docs.title: 3.4 Logistical Support
docs.text:
• An Offered Resource, Not an Obligation: My partner may, at her discretion, serve as a logistical resource for either household — driving, school pick-up/drop-off, errands, or other time-sensitive tasks. This is a voluntary extension of care, not a standing expectation.
  • Schedule Governs: Any request for logistical help is subject to her availability. A "no" or "not today" requires no explanation and carries no weight against her in any direction.
  • The Ask, Not the Assumption: Either parent may ask. Neither parent should assume. If logistics need to be counted on, they need to be confirmed — not presumed.

docs.title: 4. Communication (Signals)
docs.text:
• [SIGNAL - Hand-off]: (Partner to Kids) "I'm not going to argue with you about this. Your dad will handle it when he gets back."
  • [SIGNAL - The Respect Line]: (Paul to Kids) "You do not have to agree with [Partner], but you will treat her with respect. Speaking to her that way is completely unacceptable in this house."
  • [SIGNAL - The Timeout]: (Partner to Paul) "The house is high-stimulus right now and I'm hitting my limit. I'm going upstairs to recharge for an hour to keep my sanity."

docs.title: 5. Role Matrix
docs.text:
Action Required Paul's Role Partner's Role Imminent Danger / Injury Notified immediately; take over upon return. Intervene to ensure safety, alert Paul, handle the injury. Sibling Fighting Separate, debrief, issue consequences. Observe, maintain personal safety, stay out of the fray. Disrespect to Partner Step in immediately, defend partner, issue correction. State the boundary ("Don't speak to me that way"), hand off to Paul. Household Overwhelm Manage the kids' volume/chaos, support partner's exit. Execute the Separation Protocol without guilt.

Internal Enforcement

If I find myself hoping she will "take over" parenting tasks, or get frustrated when she steps away to preserve her sanity, that is a System Alert. I am attempting to offload my parental responsibilities onto her. I must immediately course-correct and re-assume 100% of the disciplinary load — and share what I'm feeling so we can solve it together. The underlying need may be something she can genuinely support: more personal time, help with other household load, or simply being heard. Course correction is not a solo action.

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
id: BND-REL-005
status: Draft
severity: Critical
review_frequency: Quarterly (Joint Review)
scope: Current Relationship / Blended Family

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text: The Personal Boundary Repository (PBR) is a version-controlled system for personal conduct, interpersonal expectations, and self-preservation. Each boundary is a specification: it names a rule, documents the rationale, defines implementation steps, and provides pre-scripted language for use in real-time situations.

docs.title: Why It Exists
docs.text:
This repository was built during a significant life transition — navigating divorce, establishing a co-parenting structure, protecting a current relationship from operational spillover, and managing external pressure from family and community. The common thread across all of it: a long-standing default toward conflict avoidance that consistently led to over-commitment, silent resentment, and loss of personal autonomy.

Externalizing these boundaries into a document forces clarity. A boundary that exists only as a feeling is negotiable under pressure. A boundary that is written, reviewed, and versioned is a policy.

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
The language and structure deliberately mirror software engineering practice. Boundaries are treated as specifications with IDs, severity ratings, and review schedules. Responses to violations are pre-scripted as "Signals" to reduce the cognitive load of finding words in high-friction moments. Escalation paths are defined in advance so that enforcement is a procedure, not a reaction.

This is not about being cold or clinical in relationships. It is about being prepared — so that the response in a hard moment is deliberate rather than reflexive.

docs.title: What Is Covered
docs.text:
Eight boundaries are currently active, spanning four relationship contexts:

Context Boundaries Co-Parenting Conflict Isolation, Co-Parenting & Autonomy Extended Family Information Disclosure Current Relationship Disclosure & Emotional Processing, Information Capping & Heartbeats, Role Definition & Domestic Integration Operational / Self Request Management & Response SLA, Internal Resource Audit

docs.title: 1. Goal & Philosophy
docs.text:
The goal of this repository is to provide a single source of truth for personal conduct, interpersonal expectations, and self-preservation.

  • Precision over Politeness: Use clear, declarative language.
  • Version Control: All changes to boundaries must be tracked via commit history to understand personal growth.
  • Separation of Concerns: Keep core values separate from situational implementation.

docs.title: 2. File Naming & Structure
docs.text:
• Format: All files must be .md (Markdown).
  • Naming Convention: Use kebab-case.
  • Correct: social-media-consumption.md
  • Incorrect: Social Media Consumption.md
  • Directory Mapping:
  • /core: Fundamental rights and non-negotiables.
  • /professional: Workplace and career boundaries.
  • /relational: Friends, family, and partner-specific logic.
  • /templates: Blank specs for new boundary creation.

docs.title: 3. The Boundary Specification Template
docs.text: Every boundary document must follow this standardized section hierarchy:

docs.title: 3.1 Required Sections
docs.text:
1. # Boundary Title — A high-level name for the boundary.
  2. ## 1. The Line — A single-sentence bold statement of the rule.
  3. ## 2. Rationale — The "Why." Link to core values or past incidents that necessitated this rule.
  4. ## 3. Implementation — A bulleted list of technical or physical steps taken to enforce the boundary.
  5. ## 4. Communication (Signals) — Standardized phrases to use when the boundary is tested.
  6. ## Metadata — YAML block at the end of the file tracking status and scope.

docs.title: 3.2 Metadata Block (End of File)
docs.text:
Place a fenced YAML code block at the end of each document under a ## Metadata heading:

## Metadata

```yaml
id: BND-001
status: Draft | Active | Deprecated
severity: Low | Medium | High | Critical
review_frequency: Quarterly | Annual | Event-Driven
scope: Relational | Internal | Operational
```

docs.title: The "[SIGNAL]" Callout
docs.text:
Use **[SIGNAL - Name]:** inline in bullet lists for pre-scripted responses. This reduces the cognitive load of finding the right words in the moment.

[SIGNAL]: "I appreciate the invite, but I don't do social events on weeknights to ensure I get enough sleep."

docs.title: The "[!WARNING]" Enforcement Block
docs.text:
For high-severity boundaries, use a MkDocs admonition block to define what happens if the line is crossed:

!!! warning "Enforcement Step"
    If [Person] continues to [Action] after the Signal is given, I will remove myself from the environment immediately.

docs.title: Typography Rules
docs.text:
• Keywords: Use bold for absolute negatives (e.g., No, Never, Will not).
  • Internal Notes: Use italics for personal reflections or notes to self regarding the difficulty of the boundary.

docs.title: 5. Vocabulary Standards
docs.text:
To maintain the "Code Project" feel, avoid vague or deferential language:

Avoid Use Instead I try not to... I do not... I would prefer if... I require... Maybe we can... The policy is... I feel like... The boundary is...
